Operator notes for this run:
## Operator note — 2026-03-28T18:27:01.073Z

Refer to health-check/health-check.txt and address

These notes are authoritative user steering for this run. Follow them unless they directly conflict with the repository state or the controller's explicit task.

Parent conversation handoff summary:
Parent conversation handoff summary
Summary model: anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
Source items: 265

Overview:
ST6 is an enterprise software company enabling weekly commit-based planning with AI assistance. The session discussed how to make AI integral to the product (not bolted-on) and reviewed an evaluation pipeline for AI quality. We concluded the next milestone is implementing enforceable AI quality thresholds via: adding a critical field to eval cases, wiring two existing LLM judge prompts (title-quality, criteria-quality) into the PromptEvalRunner, committing the most recent eval run as a regression baseline, creating an eval-thresholds.json config, building a Node.js enforcement script that compares results against thresholds and baseline, and integrating that into CI.

Settled decisions:
- Judge thresholds must be 3-layer: deterministic gates first (schema+case pass rate), LLM judge absolute thresholds second, regression relative thresholds third.
- Split judge thresholds by risk: high-stakes features (risk signals, insights, RAG) get stricter thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.90) than assistive features (draft, lint, suggestions) which get more permissive thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.85).
- Deterministic thresholds are hard-gates that block CI; judge and regression thresholds initially soft-gate (report but don't block) until stable.
- Use eval-baseline.json as regression anchor committed to the repo. When eval runs, the enforcement script compares the latest results to both the config thresholds and the baseline snapshot.
- Judge scoring in the eval pipeline is optional: if OPENROUTER_API_KEY is not set, judge dimensions are skipped, not failed.
- The enforcement script will be a standalone Node.js script (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins, runnable outside Gradle.
- After step 2, the title-quality and criteria-quality judge prompts will be wired into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases to produce real judge scores for calibration.

Constraints / must-remember items:
- All work is backend-test-only for steps 1-2; do not modify any main or frontend source files during those steps.
- Java code must pass spotlessCheck (Eclipse JDT formatter).
- PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure must be preserved, not replaced.
- FaithfulnessEvaluator.java (runtime judge, separate from eval pipeline) must not be modified.
- Eval runner is @Tag("eval") and excluded from normal test runs — preserve that behavior.
- Judge scoring must be optional: skip if provider unavailable (no API key), don't fail.
- Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/ — keep that path.
- Step 3 requires exactly 6 test cases marked as critical: 3 in draft-assist (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008) and 3 in commit-lint (lint-001, lint-003, lint-004).
- Regression thresholds must be configurable via eval-thresholds.json, not hardcoded in script logic.

Open questions:
- Should the enforcement script exit 0 or 1 when judge scores are unavailable (API key missing)? Currently plan says skip those checks and pass on deterministic, but CI teams may want a clear signal.
- The eval-baseline.json in step 3 references an old run timestamp (2026-03-26). Should we run a fresh eval before committing baseline to ensure the cases are stable?
- How should the enforcement script integrate with existing Gradle eval tasks — should it be invoked inside build.gradle or externally in CI?
- Do we want the threshold config to be versioned per prompt-version (e.g., separate thresholds for commit-draft-assist-v1 vs v2), or single global thresholds for all versions of a suggestion type?

Use this handoff as background context. If it conflicts with the explicit task below or the current repository state, prioritize the explicit task and repo state.

Plan overview:
{
  "goal": "Implement enforceable AI quality thresholds by: (1) adding a `critical` field to eval cases, (2) wiring the existing title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judge prompts into `PromptEvalRunner` so draft-assist cases collect real judge scores, (3) committing the most recent eval output as a regression baseline and creating an `eval-thresholds.json` config, (4) building a threshold enforcement script that reads eval results and compares against the config + baseline, (5) wiring that script into the GitLab `eval:llm-judge` job, and (6) adding unit tests for the enforcement script. The result is a pipeline where deterministic checks hard-gate, judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting, and regressions against baseline are detected. Constraints: backend-only for steps 1-3 (no frontend modifications); Java must pass spotlessCheck; preserve existing PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure; judge scoring is optional (skip if no OPENROUTER_API_KEY); do NOT modify FaithfulnessEvaluator.java; eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/; enforcement script is standalone Node.js (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins.",
  "totalSteps": 6,
  "stepTitles": [
    "1. Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON",
    "2. Wire title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judges into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases",
    "3. Commit the most recent eval run as baseline and create eval-thresholds.json",
    "4. Build the threshold enforcement script",
    "5. Split CI into two jobs: eval run (manual/allow_failure) + threshold check (hard-gate), and add npm scripts",
    "6. Add unit tests for the enforcement script and verify end-to-end"
  ]
}

Step 1: Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON
{
  "id": "step-1",
  "title": "Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON",
  "description": "Add an optional `critical` boolean field to `backend/src/test/java/com/weeklycommit/ai/eval/EvalCase.java` record. Jackson 2.18.3 defaults missing primitive record fields to `false`, so no extra annotation is needed beyond the existing `@JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown = true)`.\n\nUpdate `EvalResult.java` (a class, not a record, with a 5-arg constructor: `(String caseId, String description, String promptVersion, boolean schemaValid, String rawOutput)`) to add `boolean critical` as a 6th constructor parameter. Add a `isCritical()` getter. Include `critical` in the `toString()` output and ensure it is serialized in the JSON report (Jackson will pick up `isCritical()` automatically as a property).\n\nUpdate all 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites in `PromptEvalRunner.java` (one per eval type: commitDraftAssist, commitLint, rcdoSuggest, riskSignal, reconcileAssist, ragQuery) to pass `evalCase.critical()` as the 6th argument.\n\nUpdate fixture JSON files to mark critical cases:\n- `backend/src/test/resources/eval/commit-draft-assist/cases.json` — add `\"critical\": true` to cases `draft-001`, `draft-004`, `draft-008`\n- `backend/src/test/resources/eval/commit-lint/cases.json` — add `\"critical\": true` to cases `lint-001`, `lint-003`, `lint-004`\n\nScope: Only modify files in `backend/src/test/`. Do NOT modify any file in `backend/src/main/` or `frontend/`.",
  "requiredChecks": [
    "build"
  ]
}

This is relay round 2. Another agent has already worked on this step.

You are in VERIFICATION MODE. Your default action is to APPROVE.
Focus your review on the DIFF — the changes made in the previous round.
Only make changes if there is a MATERIAL defect: failing check, missing step requirement, logic bug, or security issue.
Style preferences, alternative approaches, comment wording, and optional polish are NOT grounds for changes.

If all checks pass and the step requirements are met, approve immediately.
Log anything else you notice as observations — do not act on them.

Previous agent's notes:
Exactly the right 6 cases are marked critical (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008, lint-001, lint-003, lint-004). All other cases default to `false` (not present in JSON, handled by `@JsonIgnoreProperties` + Jackson's record deserialization).

## Summary

**What I implemented for Step 1:**

1. **`EvalCase.java`** — Added `boolean critical` as the 6th component of the record. Jackson 2.18+ record deserialization defaults missing primitive fields to `false`, so no annotation needed beyond the existing `@JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown = true)`.

2. **`EvalResult.java`** — Added `boolean critical` field, updated the constructor to accept it as 6th parameter, added `isCritical()` getter, and included `critical=` in `toString()`. Jackson will serialize `isCritical()` as the `critical` property automatically.

3. **`PromptEvalRunner.java`** — Updated all 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites (commitDraftAssist, commitLint, rcdoSuggest, riskSignal, reconcileAssist, ragQuery) to pass `evalCase.critical()` as the 6th argument.

4. **`commit-draft-assist/cases.json`** — Added `"critical": true` to `draft-001`, `draft-004`, `draft-008`.

5. **`commit-lint/cases.json`** — Added `"critical": true` to `lint-001`, `lint-003`, `lint-004`.

6. **Ran `spotlessApply`** to fix formatting violations (including pre-existing ones in the working tree). Build now passes cleanly.

**Build result:** `BUILD SUCCESSFUL`

```observations
- [medium] backend/src/main/java/com/weeklycommit/ai/evidence/LineageQueryService.java, StructuredEvidenceService.java: Untracked files in working directory (from a previous session), not committed to git. These caused pre-existing spotless violations that I fixed via spotlessApply.
- [medium] backend/src/main/java/com/weeklycommit/ai/controller/AiController.java, backend/src/main/java/com/weeklycommit/domain/repository/UserWeekFactRepository.java: These are modified in the working tree relative to HEAD but aren't part of any current step. They should be committed in the appropriate step.
- [low] The working tree's PromptEvalRunner.java (all 6 eval types) is not committed to HEAD — HEAD only has the first 2 eval types. The working tree contains the full implementation that includes rcdo-suggest, risk-signal, reconcile-assist, and rag-query methods. These are likely from a prior session and need to be committed.
```

Verdict: changes_made

content.text:
# Plan Health Check
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
Verdict: ⚠️ issues found

## Summary

The plan is well-structured and covers all goal requirements with no gaps. File paths, API references, and existing code patterns are largely accurate — the plan author clearly studied the codebase. However, there is one critical execution bug: Step 6 appends `node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` to the npm `test` script, but the preceding `cd backend` in the same command chain means the working directory is `backend/` when the node command runs, causing it to look for test files in the wrong directory. This will cause the `unit` requiredCheck to fail. Additionally, Step 4's type classification logic for applying `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` thresholds is underspecified (eval results don't have an explicit type field), and the baseline contains only 18 of 52 possible cases, making regression comparisons across different population sizes ambiguous. The spotless formatting risk on Step 2's large code addition is manageable but noteworthy.

## Goal Requirements Covered

- ✅ Adding a `critical` field to eval cases (Step 1)
- ✅ Wiring title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judge prompts into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases (Step 2)
- ✅ Committing most recent eval output as regression baseline (Step 3)
- ✅ Creating eval-thresholds.json config (Step 3)
- ✅ Building threshold enforcement script (Step 4)
- ✅ Wiring enforcement script into GitLab eval pipeline (Step 5)
- ✅ Adding unit tests for enforcement script (Step 6)
- ✅ Backend-only constraint for steps 1-3
- ✅ Java must pass spotlessCheck (build requiredCheck includes spotless)
- ✅ Preserve existing PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure
- ✅ Judge scoring optional / skip if no OPENROUTER_API_KEY
- ✅ Do NOT modify FaithfulnessEvaluator.java
- ✅ Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/
- ✅ Enforcement script is standalone Node.js using only Node 20 built-ins
- ✅ Deterministic checks hard-gate via non-allow_failure threshold-check job
- ✅ Judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting
- ✅ Regression detection against baseline

## Step Risk Assessment

### 🟢 step-1: Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON — low risk
- The plan claims Jackson 2.18.3 defaults missing primitive record fields to `false` without extra annotations. This is correct for Jackson 2.18+ record deserialization, but the ObjectMapper in PromptEvalRunner is a plain `new ObjectMapper()` (line 50), not Spring's auto-configured one. Record support is built into Jackson 2.15+ core, so this should still work, but the agent should verify.
- The plan says 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites. Confirmed exactly 6 at lines 108, 170, 220, 275, 330, 377. Correct.

### 🟡 step-2: Wire title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judges into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases — medium risk
- Large code addition (~80-100 lines) to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. The agent must format with `./gradlew spotlessApply` or match Eclipse conventions exactly.
- The plan specifies judge score dimension names (`titleJudge_clarity`, `titleJudge_specificity`, etc.) but the actual judge prompt responses use keys without the prefix (`clarity`, `specificity`, etc.). The agent must correctly prepend the `titleJudge_` and `criteriaJudge_` prefixes when calling `addScore()`.
- The `callJudge()` helper using raw `java.net.http.HttpClient` needs to construct the OpenRouter chat completions request body correctly (model, messages array with role/content). This is non-trivial and the plan doesn't show the exact request format. Agent will need to reference OpenRouter/OpenAI API docs or the existing OpenRouterAiProvider code (which is in src/main and cannot be modified but can be read).
- The plan says to read judge prompts from classpath: `eval/judge-prompts/title-quality-judge.txt` and `eval/judge-prompts/criteria-quality-judge.txt`. These files exist at the correct paths. Confirmed.
- Placeholder names in the plan match the actual prompt template files. `{originalTitle}`, `{suggestedTitle}`, `{chessPiece}` for title judge; `{commitTitle}`, `{chessPiece}`, `{suggestedCriteria}` for criteria judge. Confirmed.

### 🟡 step-3: Commit the most recent eval run as baseline and create eval-thresholds.json — medium risk
- The baseline file `backend/build/eval-results/eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` exists locally but is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent needs to copy its content to `eval-baseline.json` at repo root, which is NOT gitignored. The file is accessible on disk but may confuse agents that check git status.
- The baseline only contains 18 results (12 draft-assist + 6 lint). The fixture files define 52 total cases across 6 eval types. Future eval runs will have ~52 results. The regression comparison in step 4 must handle different population sizes, but step 3 doesn't call this out.
- The thresholds config references `judge_high_stakes.applies_to: ['RISK_SIGNAL', 'TEAM_INSIGHT', 'PERSONAL_INSIGHT', 'RAG_QUERY']` and `judge_assistive.applies_to: ['COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST', ...]`. But the eval results don't have an explicit 'type' field — only `caseId` prefix (e.g., `draft-`, `lint-`) and `promptVersion` (e.g., `commit-draft-assist-v1`). The enforcement script (step 4) must infer type from these, which is underspecified.

### 🟡 step-4: Build the threshold enforcement script — medium risk
- The plan says to use `path.resolve(__dirname, '..')` for repo root. This works for `scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` since `__dirname` is `<repo>/scripts/`. Correct.
- The eval result JSON does NOT have a per-result `passed` boolean field. There is only a top-level `passed` count. The plan correctly describes computing pass/fail from `schemaValid` and `checks`, but the agent must implement this rather than expecting a `passed` field.
- Type classification for `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` threshold application is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in `eval-thresholds.json` reference type names like `COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST` but eval results use `promptVersion: 'commit-draft-assist-v1'` or caseId prefix `draft-`. The plan doesn't specify the mapping. For the initial implementation, title/criteria judge scores only exist on draft-assist cases so global means work, but faithfulness thresholds require type splitting.
- The baseline has 18 cases; future runs have 52. Regression checks comparing overall pass rates will compare different populations. The script should ideally compare per-caseId pass/fail for regression, but the plan describes high-level rate comparisons.

### 🟢 step-5: Split CI into two jobs: eval run (manual/allow_failure) + threshold check (hard-gate), and add npm scripts — low risk
- The plan says `eval:threshold-check` should have `rules` matching `eval:llm-judge` with `when: on_success`. In GitLab CI, with `needs: ['eval:llm-judge']` and `eval:llm-judge` being manual, the threshold-check job will be in 'created' state until the manual job completes. This is correct behavior.
- The `node:20-bookworm` image does not have npm/node_modules from the repo. But the script uses only Node 20 built-ins (fs, path, etc.) so no `npm ci` is needed. Correct.
- Adding `eval:run` and `eval:check` npm scripts to package.json is straightforward.

### 🔴 step-6: Add unit tests for the enforcement script and verify end-to-end — high risk

⚠️ **Checks will fail:** The `unit` npm script (`npm run test`) ends with `cd backend && ./gradlew test`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` runs from the `backend/` directory, so the glob resolves to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The node command will fail with 'no test files found' or similar error.
- CRITICAL: The current `test` npm script is `npm run test -w frontend -- --run && npm run test -w @weekly-commit/shared -- --run && cd backend && ./gradlew test`. After `cd backend && ./gradlew test` completes, the shell working directory is `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` will resolve the glob relative to `backend/`, looking for `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The `unit` requiredCheck will FAIL. Fix: must include `cd ..` before the node command, e.g., `&& cd .. && node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js`.
- The test file uses `require('../eval-threshold-check.js')` to import exported functions. Step 4 must have the CommonJS exports and `require.main === module` guard working correctly for this to succeed. This is a cross-step dependency that is properly sequenced (step 4 before step 6).
- Node 20's built-in test runner (`node:test`) uses `describe`/`it` or `test()` syntax. The agent needs to use the correct API. This is well-documented but agents sometimes confuse it with Jest.

## Critical Issues (will cause failure)

- 🔴 Step 6: npm `test` script `cd backend` leaves cwd in `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` resolves the glob to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. Must insert `&& cd ..` before the node command. The `unit` requiredCheck WILL fail as written.

## Warnings (may cause problems)

- 🟡 Step 4: Type classification for judge thresholds is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in eval-thresholds.json reference types like 'COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST' but eval results only have `promptVersion` ('commit-draft-assist-v1') and caseId prefix ('draft-'). Agent must infer the mapping.
- 🟡 Step 3: The baseline `eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` only has 18 results (lint + draft-assist), while fixture files define 52 cases across 6 eval types. Regression pass-rate comparison across different population sizes is misleading unless done per-case.
- 🟡 Step 2: ~80-100 lines of new code (HTTP client, JSON parsing, template substitution) added to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. Agent should run `./gradlew spotlessApply` after editing.
- 🟡 Step 3: The source baseline file at `backend/build/eval-results/` is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent must copy content from the local disk file, not from git. This works but could confuse agents that rely on git-tracked files only.

Summary model: anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
Source items: 265

Overview:
ST6 is an enterprise software company enabling weekly commit-based planning with AI assistance. The session discussed how to make AI integral to the product (not bolted-on) and reviewed an evaluation pipeline for AI quality. We concluded the next milestone is implementing enforceable AI quality thresholds via: adding a critical field to eval cases, wiring two existing LLM judge prompts (title-quality, criteria-quality) into the PromptEvalRunner, committing the most recent eval run as a regression baseline, creating an eval-thresholds.json config, building a Node.js enforcement script that compares results against thresholds and baseline, and integrating that into CI.

Settled decisions:
- Judge thresholds must be 3-layer: deterministic gates first (schema+case pass rate), LLM judge absolute thresholds second, regression relative thresholds third.
- Split judge thresholds by risk: high-stakes features (risk signals, insights, RAG) get stricter thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.90) than assistive features (draft, lint, suggestions) which get more permissive thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.85).
- Deterministic thresholds are hard-gates that block CI; judge and regression thresholds initially soft-gate (report but don't block) until stable.
- Use eval-baseline.json as regression anchor committed to the repo. When eval runs, the enforcement script compares the latest results to both the config thresholds and the baseline snapshot.
- Judge scoring in the eval pipeline is optional: if OPENROUTER_API_KEY is not set, judge dimensions are skipped, not failed.
- The enforcement script will be a standalone Node.js script (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins, runnable outside Gradle.
- After step 2, the title-quality and criteria-quality judge prompts will be wired into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases to produce real judge scores for calibration.

Constraints / must-remember items:
- All work is backend-test-only for steps 1-2; do not modify any main or frontend source files during those steps.
- Java code must pass spotlessCheck (Eclipse JDT formatter).
- PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure must be preserved, not replaced.
- FaithfulnessEvaluator.java (runtime judge, separate from eval pipeline) must not be modified.
- Eval runner is @Tag("eval") and excluded from normal test runs — preserve that behavior.
- Judge scoring must be optional: skip if provider unavailable (no API key), don't fail.
- Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/ — keep that path.
- Step 3 requires exactly 6 test cases marked as critical: 3 in draft-assist (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008) and 3 in commit-lint (lint-001, lint-003, lint-004).
- Regression thresholds must be configurable via eval-thresholds.json, not hardcoded in script logic.

Open questions:
- Should the enforcement script exit 0 or 1 when judge scores are unavailable (API key missing)? Currently plan says skip those checks and pass on deterministic, but CI teams may want a clear signal.
- The eval-baseline.json in step 3 references an old run timestamp (2026-03-26). Should we run a fresh eval before committing baseline to ensure the cases are stable?
- How should the enforcement script integrate with existing Gradle eval tasks — should it be invoked inside build.gradle or externally in CI?
- Do we want the threshold config to be versioned per prompt-version (e.g., separate thresholds for commit-draft-assist-v1 vs v2), or single global thresholds for all versions of a suggestion type?

Use this handoff as background context. If it conflicts with the explicit task below or the current repository state, prioritize the explicit task and repo state.

The result is a pipeline where deterministic checks hard-gate, judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting, and regressions against baseline are detected. ## Summary

**What I implemented for Step 2:**

1. **New imports** — Added `java.net.URI`, `java.net.http.HttpClient`, `java.net.http.HttpRequest`, `java.net.http.HttpResponse`, and `java.nio.charset.StandardCharsets` to the import section.

2. **LLM judge calls inside `evaluateCommitDraftAssist()`** — Inserted two guarded judge-call blocks inside the existing `if (schemaValid && output != null)` block (so they automatically inherit the `providerAvailable` guard via early return at method top):
   - **Title judge**: fires when `suggestedTitle` is present and non-blank; substitutes `{originalTitle}`, `{suggestedTitle}`, `{chessPiece}` from the case input; stores 5 scores prefixed `titleJudge_`
   - **Criteria judge**: fires when `suggestedSuccessCriteria` is present and non-blank; substitutes `{commitTitle}`, `{chessPiece}`, `{suggestedCriteria}`; stores 4 scores prefixed `criteriaJudge_`
   - Both are wrapped in `try/catch` so failures log a warning and never fail the test

3. **`loadJudgePrompt(String)` helper** — Reads a classpath resource as UTF-8 text; throws `IllegalArgumentException` if not found.

4. **`callJudge(String)` helper** — Direct HTTP POST to `https://openrouter.ai/api/v1/chat/completions` using `java.net.http.HttpClient`; reads `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` and `OPENROUTER_MODEL` from env; strips markdown fences from the response content before JSON parsing.

content.text:
# Plan Health Check
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
Verdict: ⚠️ issues found

## Summary

The plan is well-structured and covers all goal requirements with no gaps. File paths, API references, and existing code patterns are largely accurate — the plan author clearly studied the codebase. However, there is one critical execution bug: Step 6 appends `node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` to the npm `test` script, but the preceding `cd backend` in the same command chain means the working directory is `backend/` when the node command runs, causing it to look for test files in the wrong directory. This will cause the `unit` requiredCheck to fail. Additionally, Step 4's type classification logic for applying `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` thresholds is underspecified (eval results don't have an explicit type field), and the baseline contains only 18 of 52 possible cases, making regression comparisons across different population sizes ambiguous. The spotless formatting risk on Step 2's large code addition is manageable but noteworthy.

## Goal Requirements Covered

- ✅ Adding a `critical` field to eval cases (Step 1)
- ✅ Wiring title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judge prompts into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases (Step 2)
- ✅ Committing most recent eval output as regression baseline (Step 3)
- ✅ Creating eval-thresholds.json config (Step 3)
- ✅ Building threshold enforcement script (Step 4)
- ✅ Wiring enforcement script into GitLab eval pipeline (Step 5)
- ✅ Adding unit tests for enforcement script (Step 6)
- ✅ Backend-only constraint for steps 1-3
- ✅ Java must pass spotlessCheck (build requiredCheck includes spotless)
- ✅ Preserve existing PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure
- ✅ Judge scoring optional / skip if no OPENROUTER_API_KEY
- ✅ Do NOT modify FaithfulnessEvaluator.java
- ✅ Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/
- ✅ Enforcement script is standalone Node.js using only Node 20 built-ins
- ✅ Deterministic checks hard-gate via non-allow_failure threshold-check job
- ✅ Judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting
- ✅ Regression detection against baseline

## Step Risk Assessment

### 🟢 step-1: Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON — low risk
- The plan claims Jackson 2.18.3 defaults missing primitive record fields to `false` without extra annotations. This is correct for Jackson 2.18+ record deserialization, but the ObjectMapper in PromptEvalRunner is a plain `new ObjectMapper()` (line 50), not Spring's auto-configured one. Record support is built into Jackson 2.15+ core, so this should still work, but the agent should verify.
- The plan says 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites. Confirmed exactly 6 at lines 108, 170, 220, 275, 330, 377. Correct.

### 🟡 step-2: Wire title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judges into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases — medium risk
- Large code addition (~80-100 lines) to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. The agent must format with `./gradlew spotlessApply` or match Eclipse conventions exactly.
- The plan specifies judge score dimension names (`titleJudge_clarity`, `titleJudge_specificity`, etc.) but the actual judge prompt responses use keys without the prefix (`clarity`, `specificity`, etc.). The agent must correctly prepend the `titleJudge_` and `criteriaJudge_` prefixes when calling `addScore()`.
- The `callJudge()` helper using raw `java.net.http.HttpClient` needs to construct the OpenRouter chat completions request body correctly (model, messages array with role/content). This is non-trivial and the plan doesn't show the exact request format. Agent will need to reference OpenRouter/OpenAI API docs or the existing OpenRouterAiProvider code (which is in src/main and cannot be modified but can be read).
- The plan says to read judge prompts from classpath: `eval/judge-prompts/title-quality-judge.txt` and `eval/judge-prompts/criteria-quality-judge.txt`. These files exist at the correct paths. Confirmed.
- Placeholder names in the plan match the actual prompt template files. `{originalTitle}`, `{suggestedTitle}`, `{chessPiece}` for title judge; `{commitTitle}`, `{chessPiece}`, `{suggestedCriteria}` for criteria judge. Confirmed.

### 🟡 step-3: Commit the most recent eval run as baseline and create eval-thresholds.json — medium risk
- The baseline file `backend/build/eval-results/eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` exists locally but is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent needs to copy its content to `eval-baseline.json` at repo root, which is NOT gitignored. The file is accessible on disk but may confuse agents that check git status.
- The baseline only contains 18 results (12 draft-assist + 6 lint). The fixture files define 52 total cases across 6 eval types. Future eval runs will have ~52 results. The regression comparison in step 4 must handle different population sizes, but step 3 doesn't call this out.
- The thresholds config references `judge_high_stakes.applies_to: ['RISK_SIGNAL', 'TEAM_INSIGHT', 'PERSONAL_INSIGHT', 'RAG_QUERY']` and `judge_assistive.applies_to: ['COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST', ...]`. But the eval results don't have an explicit 'type' field — only `caseId` prefix (e.g., `draft-`, `lint-`) and `promptVersion` (e.g., `commit-draft-assist-v1`). The enforcement script (step 4) must infer type from these, which is underspecified.

### 🟡 step-4: Build the threshold enforcement script — medium risk
- The plan says to use `path.resolve(__dirname, '..')` for repo root. This works for `scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` since `__dirname` is `<repo>/scripts/`. Correct.
- The eval result JSON does NOT have a per-result `passed` boolean field. There is only a top-level `passed` count. The plan correctly describes computing pass/fail from `schemaValid` and `checks`, but the agent must implement this rather than expecting a `passed` field.
- Type classification for `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` threshold application is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in `eval-thresholds.json` reference type names like `COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST` but eval results use `promptVersion: 'commit-draft-assist-v1'` or caseId prefix `draft-`. The plan doesn't specify the mapping. For the initial implementation, title/criteria judge scores only exist on draft-assist cases so global means work, but faithfulness thresholds require type splitting.
- The baseline has 18 cases; future runs have 52. Regression checks comparing overall pass rates will compare different populations. The script should ideally compare per-caseId pass/fail for regression, but the plan describes high-level rate comparisons.

### 🟢 step-5: Split CI into two jobs: eval run (manual/allow_failure) + threshold check (hard-gate), and add npm scripts — low risk
- The plan says `eval:threshold-check` should have `rules` matching `eval:llm-judge` with `when: on_success`. In GitLab CI, with `needs: ['eval:llm-judge']` and `eval:llm-judge` being manual, the threshold-check job will be in 'created' state until the manual job completes. This is correct behavior.
- The `node:20-bookworm` image does not have npm/node_modules from the repo. But the script uses only Node 20 built-ins (fs, path, etc.) so no `npm ci` is needed. Correct.
- Adding `eval:run` and `eval:check` npm scripts to package.json is straightforward.

### 🔴 step-6: Add unit tests for the enforcement script and verify end-to-end — high risk

⚠️ **Checks will fail:** The `unit` npm script (`npm run test`) ends with `cd backend && ./gradlew test`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` runs from the `backend/` directory, so the glob resolves to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The node command will fail with 'no test files found' or similar error.
- CRITICAL: The current `test` npm script is `npm run test -w frontend -- --run && npm run test -w @weekly-commit/shared -- --run && cd backend && ./gradlew test`. After `cd backend && ./gradlew test` completes, the shell working directory is `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` will resolve the glob relative to `backend/`, looking for `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The `unit` requiredCheck will FAIL. Fix: must include `cd ..` before the node command, e.g., `&& cd .. && node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js`.
- The test file uses `require('../eval-threshold-check.js')` to import exported functions. Step 4 must have the CommonJS exports and `require.main === module` guard working correctly for this to succeed. This is a cross-step dependency that is properly sequenced (step 4 before step 6).
- Node 20's built-in test runner (`node:test`) uses `describe`/`it` or `test()` syntax. The agent needs to use the correct API. This is well-documented but agents sometimes confuse it with Jest.

## Critical Issues (will cause failure)

- 🔴 Step 6: npm `test` script `cd backend` leaves cwd in `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` resolves the glob to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. Must insert `&& cd ..` before the node command. The `unit` requiredCheck WILL fail as written.

## Warnings (may cause problems)

- 🟡 Step 4: Type classification for judge thresholds is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in eval-thresholds.json reference types like 'COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST' but eval results only have `promptVersion` ('commit-draft-assist-v1') and caseId prefix ('draft-'). Agent must infer the mapping.
- 🟡 Step 3: The baseline `eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` only has 18 results (lint + draft-assist), while fixture files define 52 cases across 6 eval types. Regression pass-rate comparison across different population sizes is misleading unless done per-case.
- 🟡 Step 2: ~80-100 lines of new code (HTTP client, JSON parsing, template substitution) added to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. Agent should run `./gradlew spotlessApply` after editing.
- 🟡 Step 3: The source baseline file at `backend/build/eval-results/` is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent must copy content from the local disk file, not from git. This works but could confuse agents that rely on git-tracked files only.

**Inspecting approval criteria**

I'm figuring out if I should approve or suggest changes after inspecting the full file for any material defects. I see potential issues like the callJudge function not checking HTTP status or missing content, but it’s wrapped in a try/catch, which might explain missing scores. The step mentions that scoring is optional, which raises questions about acceptability. If a response has markdown fences with a json tag, I need to check the stripping logic. Parsing the response into JSON content is necessary, but handling it as an array? I'll verify the judge prompts exist and confirm if the build passes. I should also inspect if the parameterized structure is preserved. There’s a concern about callJudge reading the environment again despite already checking if the provider is available. But it seems no issue—just a minor note on a static review regarding InputStream closure.

**Identifying potential defects**

I’m spotting a few potential material defects. OpenRouter might return HTTP 4xx/5xx statuses with a JSON error object, causing responseNode.path("choices").path(0) to produce an empty string, which leads to objectMapper.readTree("") failing and silently skipping the error. There’s also a question about whether the judge prompt needs a system prompt since the step says to use a filled template as a user message. Furthermore, the evaluateCommitDraftAssist adds judge scores only if schemaValid and output isn't null, which is fine if the requirements are met. I’m thinking I might not need a providerAvailable guard around callJudge since the top method already returns early if there’s no provider. I'll check for a potential bug with `input.path("title").asText("")`, ensuring the commit title substitution works correctly and confirming quality prompt file placeholders.

content.text:
You are evaluating the quality of an AI-suggested commit title improvement for a weekly planning tool.

Original title: "{originalTitle}"
Suggested title: "{suggestedTitle}"
Chess piece: {chessPiece} (KING = mission-critical, QUEEN = highest-leverage, ROOK = major deliverable, BISHOP = enabling work, KNIGHT = exploratory, PAWN = small task)

Rate the suggestion on these criteria (each 0.0 to 1.0):

1. clarity: Is the suggested title clearer about what will be delivered? Does it describe an OUTCOME not just an ACTIVITY?
2. specificity: Does it contain concrete deliverables, not vague words like "stuff", "things", "work on", "various"?
3. appropriate_scope: Does it describe work achievable in a single week? (not too broad, not trivially small for its chess piece)
4. improvement: Is it meaningfully better than the original? If the original was already good, is the suggestion unnecessary (score 0)?
5. professional_tone: Is the language appropriate for a work planning system? No emoji, no slang, no exclamation marks.

Respond with ONLY a JSON object:
{"clarity": 0.8, "specificity": 0.7, "appropriate_scope": 0.9, "improvement": 0.6, "professional_tone": 0.95}

You are evaluating the quality of AI-suggested success criteria for a weekly planning commit.

Commit title: "{commitTitle}"
Chess piece: {chessPiece}
Suggested success criteria: "{suggestedCriteria}"

Rate the suggestion on these criteria (each 0.0 to 1.0):

1. measurable: Can you objectively determine if this criterion is met? Look for specific numbers, states, or verifiable conditions. Vague criteria like "done well" or "improved" score low.
2. completeness: Does it cover the key aspects of the commit's deliverable? A KING commit needs more comprehensive criteria than a PAWN.
3. achievable_in_one_week: Is this realistically achievable within one week given the described scope?
4. testable: Could a teammate verify whether this criterion was met without subjective judgment?

Respond with ONLY a JSON object:
{"measurable": 0.8, "completeness": 0.7, "achievable_in_one_week": 0.9, "testable": 0.85}

Overview:
ST6 is an enterprise software company enabling weekly commit-based planning with AI assistance. The session discussed how to make AI integral to the product (not bolted-on) and reviewed an evaluation pipeline for AI quality. We concluded the next milestone is implementing enforceable AI quality thresholds via: adding a critical field to eval cases, wiring two existing LLM judge prompts (title-quality, criteria-quality) into the PromptEvalRunner, committing the most recent eval run as a regression baseline, creating an eval-thresholds.json config, building a Node.js enforcement script that compares results against thresholds and baseline, and integrating that into CI.

Settled decisions:
- Judge thresholds must be 3-layer: deterministic gates first (schema+case pass rate), LLM judge absolute thresholds second, regression relative thresholds third.
- Split judge thresholds by risk: high-stakes features (risk signals, insights, RAG) get stricter thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.90) than assistive features (draft, lint, suggestions) which get more permissive thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.85).
- Deterministic thresholds are hard-gates that block CI; judge and regression thresholds initially soft-gate (report but don't block) until stable.
- Use eval-baseline.json as regression anchor committed to the repo. When eval runs, the enforcement script compares the latest results to both the config thresholds and the baseline snapshot.
- Judge scoring in the eval pipeline is optional: if OPENROUTER_API_KEY is not set, judge dimensions are skipped, not failed.
- The enforcement script will be a standalone Node.js script (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins, runnable outside Gradle.
- After step 2, the title-quality and criteria-quality judge prompts will be wired into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases to produce real judge scores for calibration.

Constraints / must-remember items:
- All work is backend-test-only for steps 1-2; do not modify any main or frontend source files during those steps.
- Java code must pass spotlessCheck (Eclipse JDT formatter).
- PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure must be preserved, not replaced.
- FaithfulnessEvaluator.java (runtime judge, separate from eval pipeline) must not be modified.
- Eval runner is @Tag("eval") and excluded from normal test runs — preserve that behavior.
- Judge scoring must be optional: skip if provider unavailable (no API key), don't fail.
- Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/ — keep that path.
- Step 3 requires exactly 6 test cases marked as critical: 3 in draft-assist (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008) and 3 in commit-lint (lint-001, lint-003, lint-004).
- Regression thresholds must be configurable via eval-thresholds.json, not hardcoded in script logic.

Open questions:
- Should the enforcement script exit 0 or 1 when judge scores are unavailable (API key missing)? Currently plan says skip those checks and pass on deterministic, but CI teams may want a clear signal.
- The eval-baseline.json in step 3 references an old run timestamp (2026-03-26). Should we run a fresh eval before committing baseline to ensure the cases are stable?
- How should the enforcement script integrate with existing Gradle eval tasks — should it be invoked inside build.gradle or externally in CI?
- Do we want the threshold config to be versioned per prompt-version (e.g., separate thresholds for commit-draft-assist-v1 vs v2), or single global thresholds for all versions of a suggestion type?

Use this handoff as background context. If it conflicts with the explicit task below or the current repository state, prioritize the explicit task and repo state.

# Plan Health Check
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
Verdict: ⚠️ issues found

## Summary

The plan is well-structured and covers all goal requirements with no gaps. File paths, API references, and existing code patterns are largely accurate — the plan author clearly studied the codebase. However, there is one critical execution bug: Step 6 appends `node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` to the npm `test` script, but the preceding `cd backend` in the same command chain means the working directory is `backend/` when the node command runs, causing it to look for test files in the wrong directory. This will cause the `unit` requiredCheck to fail. Additionally, Step 4's type classification logic for applying `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` thresholds is underspecified (eval results don't have an explicit type field), and the baseline contains only 18 of 52 possible cases, making regression comparisons across different population sizes ambiguous. The spotless formatting risk on Step 2's large code addition is manageable but noteworthy.

## Goal Requirements Covered

- ✅ Adding a `critical` field to eval cases (Step 1)
- ✅ Wiring title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judge prompts into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases (Step 2)
- ✅ Committing most recent eval output as regression baseline (Step 3)
- ✅ Creating eval-thresholds.json config (Step 3)
- ✅ Building threshold enforcement script (Step 4)
- ✅ Wiring enforcement script into GitLab eval pipeline (Step 5)
- ✅ Adding unit tests for enforcement script (Step 6)
- ✅ Backend-only constraint for steps 1-3
- ✅ Java must pass spotlessCheck (build requiredCheck includes spotless)
- ✅ Preserve existing PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure
- ✅ Judge scoring optional / skip if no OPENROUTER_API_KEY
- ✅ Do NOT modify FaithfulnessEvaluator.java
- ✅ Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/
- ✅ Enforcement script is standalone Node.js using only Node 20 built-ins
- ✅ Deterministic checks hard-gate via non-allow_failure threshold-check job
- ✅ Judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting
- ✅ Regression detection against baseline

## Step Risk Assessment

### 🟢 step-1: Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON — low risk
- The plan claims Jackson 2.18.3 defaults missing primitive record fields to `false` without extra annotations. This is correct for Jackson 2.18+ record deserialization, but the ObjectMapper in PromptEvalRunner is a plain `new ObjectMapper()` (line 50), not Spring's auto-configured one. Record support is built into Jackson 2.15+ core, so this should still work, but the agent should verify.
- The plan says 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites. Confirmed exactly 6 at lines 108, 170, 220, 275, 330, 377. Correct.

### 🟡 step-2: Wire title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judges into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases — medium risk
- Large code addition (~80-100 lines) to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. The agent must format with `./gradlew spotlessApply` or match Eclipse conventions exactly.
- The plan specifies judge score dimension names (`titleJudge_clarity`, `titleJudge_specificity`, etc.) but the actual judge prompt responses use keys without the prefix (`clarity`, `specificity`, etc.). The agent must correctly prepend the `titleJudge_` and `criteriaJudge_` prefixes when calling `addScore()`.
- The `callJudge()` helper using raw `java.net.http.HttpClient` needs to construct the OpenRouter chat completions request body correctly (model, messages array with role/content). This is non-trivial and the plan doesn't show the exact request format. Agent will need to reference OpenRouter/OpenAI API docs or the existing OpenRouterAiProvider code (which is in src/main and cannot be modified but can be read).
- The plan says to read judge prompts from classpath: `eval/judge-prompts/title-quality-judge.txt` and `eval/judge-prompts/criteria-quality-judge.txt`. These files exist at the correct paths. Confirmed.
- Placeholder names in the plan match the actual prompt template files. `{originalTitle}`, `{suggestedTitle}`, `{chessPiece}` for title judge; `{commitTitle}`, `{chessPiece}`, `{suggestedCriteria}` for criteria judge. Confirmed.

### 🟡 step-3: Commit the most recent eval run as baseline and create eval-thresholds.json — medium risk
- The baseline file `backend/build/eval-results/eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` exists locally but is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent needs to copy its content to `eval-baseline.json` at repo root, which is NOT gitignored. The file is accessible on disk but may confuse agents that check git status.
- The baseline only contains 18 results (12 draft-assist + 6 lint). The fixture files define 52 total cases across 6 eval types. Future eval runs will have ~52 results. The regression comparison in step 4 must handle different population sizes, but step 3 doesn't call this out.
- The thresholds config references `judge_high_stakes.applies_to: ['RISK_SIGNAL', 'TEAM_INSIGHT', 'PERSONAL_INSIGHT', 'RAG_QUERY']` and `judge_assistive.applies_to: ['COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST', ...]`. But the eval results don't have an explicit 'type' field — only `caseId` prefix (e.g., `draft-`, `lint-`) and `promptVersion` (e.g., `commit-draft-assist-v1`). The enforcement script (step 4) must infer type from these, which is underspecified.

### 🟡 step-4: Build the threshold enforcement script — medium risk
- The plan says to use `path.resolve(__dirname, '..')` for repo root. This works for `scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` since `__dirname` is `<repo>/scripts/`. Correct.
- The eval result JSON does NOT have a per-result `passed` boolean field. There is only a top-level `passed` count. The plan correctly describes computing pass/fail from `schemaValid` and `checks`, but the agent must implement this rather than expecting a `passed` field.
- Type classification for `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` threshold application is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in `eval-thresholds.json` reference type names like `COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST` but eval results use `promptVersion: 'commit-draft-assist-v1'` or caseId prefix `draft-`. The plan doesn't specify the mapping. For the initial implementation, title/criteria judge scores only exist on draft-assist cases so global means work, but faithfulness thresholds require type splitting.
- The baseline has 18 cases; future runs have 52. Regression checks comparing overall pass rates will compare different populations. The script should ideally compare per-caseId pass/fail for regression, but the plan describes high-level rate comparisons.

### 🟢 step-5: Split CI into two jobs: eval run (manual/allow_failure) + threshold check (hard-gate), and add npm scripts — low risk
- The plan says `eval:threshold-check` should have `rules` matching `eval:llm-judge` with `when: on_success`. In GitLab CI, with `needs: ['eval:llm-judge']` and `eval:llm-judge` being manual, the threshold-check job will be in 'created' state until the manual job completes. This is correct behavior.
- The `node:20-bookworm` image does not have npm/node_modules from the repo. But the script uses only Node 20 built-ins (fs, path, etc.) so no `npm ci` is needed. Correct.
- Adding `eval:run` and `eval:check` npm scripts to package.json is straightforward.

### 🔴 step-6: Add unit tests for the enforcement script and verify end-to-end — high risk

⚠️ **Checks will fail:** The `unit` npm script (`npm run test`) ends with `cd backend && ./gradlew test`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` runs from the `backend/` directory, so the glob resolves to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The node command will fail with 'no test files found' or similar error.
- CRITICAL: The current `test` npm script is `npm run test -w frontend -- --run && npm run test -w @weekly-commit/shared -- --run && cd backend && ./gradlew test`. After `cd backend && ./gradlew test` completes, the shell working directory is `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` will resolve the glob relative to `backend/`, looking for `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The `unit` requiredCheck will FAIL. Fix: must include `cd ..` before the node command, e.g., `&& cd .. && node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js`.
- The test file uses `require('../eval-threshold-check.js')` to import exported functions. Step 4 must have the CommonJS exports and `require.main === module` guard working correctly for this to succeed. This is a cross-step dependency that is properly sequenced (step 4 before step 6).
- Node 20's built-in test runner (`node:test`) uses `describe`/`it` or `test()` syntax. The agent needs to use the correct API. This is well-documented but agents sometimes confuse it with Jest.

## Critical Issues (will cause failure)

Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` resolves the glob to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. Must insert `&& cd ..` before the node command. The `unit` requiredCheck WILL fail as written.

Operator notes for this run:
## Operator note — 2026-03-28T18:27:01.073Z

Refer to health-check/health-check.txt and address

These notes are authoritative user steering for this run. Follow them unless they directly conflict with the repository state or the controller's explicit task.

Parent conversation handoff summary:
Parent conversation handoff summary
Summary model: anthropic/claude-haiku-4-5
Source items: 265

Overview:
ST6 is an enterprise software company enabling weekly commit-based planning with AI assistance. The session discussed how to make AI integral to the product (not bolted-on) and reviewed an evaluation pipeline for AI quality. We concluded the next milestone is implementing enforceable AI quality thresholds via: adding a critical field to eval cases, wiring two existing LLM judge prompts (title-quality, criteria-quality) into the PromptEvalRunner, committing the most recent eval run as a regression baseline, creating an eval-thresholds.json config, building a Node.js enforcement script that compares results against thresholds and baseline, and integrating that into CI.

Settled decisions:
- Judge thresholds must be 3-layer: deterministic gates first (schema+case pass rate), LLM judge absolute thresholds second, regression relative thresholds third.
- Split judge thresholds by risk: high-stakes features (risk signals, insights, RAG) get stricter thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.90) than assistive features (draft, lint, suggestions) which get more permissive thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.85).
- Deterministic thresholds are hard-gates that block CI; judge and regression thresholds initially soft-gate (report but don't block) until stable.
- Use eval-baseline.json as regression anchor committed to the repo. When eval runs, the enforcement script compares the latest results to both the config thresholds and the baseline snapshot.
- Judge scoring in the eval pipeline is optional: if OPENROUTER_API_KEY is not set, judge dimensions are skipped, not failed.
- The enforcement script will be a standalone Node.js script (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins, runnable outside Gradle.
- After step 2, the title-quality and criteria-quality judge prompts will be wired into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases to produce real judge scores for calibration.

Constraints / must-remember items:
- All work is backend-test-only for steps 1-2; do not modify any main or frontend source files during those steps.
- Java code must pass spotlessCheck (Eclipse JDT formatter).
- PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure must be preserved, not replaced.
- FaithfulnessEvaluator.java (runtime judge, separate from eval pipeline) must not be modified.
- Eval runner is @Tag("eval") and excluded from normal test runs — preserve that behavior.
- Judge scoring must be optional: skip if provider unavailable (no API key), don't fail.
- Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/ — keep that path.
- Step 3 requires exactly 6 test cases marked as critical: 3 in draft-assist (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008) and 3 in commit-lint (lint-001, lint-003, lint-004).
- Regression thresholds must be configurable via eval-thresholds.json, not hardcoded in script logic.

Open questions:
- Should the enforcement script exit 0 or 1 when judge scores are unavailable (API key missing)? Currently plan says skip those checks and pass on deterministic, but CI teams may want a clear signal.
- The eval-baseline.json in step 3 references an old run timestamp (2026-03-26). Should we run a fresh eval before committing baseline to ensure the cases are stable?
- How should the enforcement script integrate with existing Gradle eval tasks — should it be invoked inside build.gradle or externally in CI?
- Do we want the threshold config to be versioned per prompt-version (e.g., separate thresholds for commit-draft-assist-v1 vs v2), or single global thresholds for all versions of a suggestion type?

# Plan Health Check
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
Verdict: ⚠️ issues found

## Summary

The plan is well-structured and covers all goal requirements with no gaps. File paths, API references, and existing code patterns are largely accurate — the plan author clearly studied the codebase. However, there is one critical execution bug: Step 6 appends `node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` to the npm `test` script, but the preceding `cd backend` in the same command chain means the working directory is `backend/` when the node command runs, causing it to look for test files in the wrong directory. This will cause the `unit` requiredCheck to fail. Additionally, Step 4's type classification logic for applying `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` thresholds is underspecified (eval results don't have an explicit type field), and the baseline contains only 18 of 52 possible cases, making regression comparisons across different population sizes ambiguous. The spotless formatting risk on Step 2's large code addition is manageable but noteworthy.

## Goal Requirements Covered

- ✅ Adding a `critical` field to eval cases (Step 1)
- ✅ Wiring title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judge prompts into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases (Step 2)
- ✅ Committing most recent eval output as regression baseline (Step 3)
- ✅ Creating eval-thresholds.json config (Step 3)
- ✅ Building threshold enforcement script (Step 4)
- ✅ Wiring enforcement script into GitLab eval pipeline (Step 5)
- ✅ Adding unit tests for enforcement script (Step 6)
- ✅ Backend-only constraint for steps 1-3
- ✅ Java must pass spotlessCheck (build requiredCheck includes spotless)
- ✅ Preserve existing PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure
- ✅ Judge scoring optional / skip if no OPENROUTER_API_KEY
- ✅ Do NOT modify FaithfulnessEvaluator.java
- ✅ Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/
- ✅ Enforcement script is standalone Node.js using only Node 20 built-ins
- ✅ Deterministic checks hard-gate via non-allow_failure threshold-check job
- ✅ Judge scores soft-gate with clear pass/fail reporting
- ✅ Regression detection against baseline

## Step Risk Assessment

### 🟢 step-1: Add `critical` field to EvalCase/EvalResult and mark critical cases in fixture JSON — low risk
- The plan claims Jackson 2.18.3 defaults missing primitive record fields to `false` without extra annotations. This is correct for Jackson 2.18+ record deserialization, but the ObjectMapper in PromptEvalRunner is a plain `new ObjectMapper()` (line 50), not Spring's auto-configured one. Record support is built into Jackson 2.15+ core, so this should still work, but the agent should verify.
- The plan says 6 `new EvalResult(...)` call sites. Confirmed exactly 6 at lines 108, 170, 220, 275, 330, 377. Correct.

### 🟡 step-2: Wire title-quality and criteria-quality LLM judges into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases — medium risk
- Large code addition (~80-100 lines) to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. The agent must format with `./gradlew spotlessApply` or match Eclipse conventions exactly.
- The plan specifies judge score dimension names (`titleJudge_clarity`, `titleJudge_specificity`, etc.) but the actual judge prompt responses use keys without the prefix (`clarity`, `specificity`, etc.). The agent must correctly prepend the `titleJudge_` and `criteriaJudge_` prefixes when calling `addScore()`.
- The `callJudge()` helper using raw `java.net.http.HttpClient` needs to construct the OpenRouter chat completions request body correctly (model, messages array with role/content). This is non-trivial and the plan doesn't show the exact request format. Agent will need to reference OpenRouter/OpenAI API docs or the existing OpenRouterAiProvider code (which is in src/main and cannot be modified but can be read).
- The plan says to read judge prompts from classpath: `eval/judge-prompts/title-quality-judge.txt` and `eval/judge-prompts/criteria-quality-judge.txt`. These files exist at the correct paths. Confirmed.
- Placeholder names in the plan match the actual prompt template files. `{originalTitle}`, `{suggestedTitle}`, `{chessPiece}` for title judge; `{commitTitle}`, `{chessPiece}`, `{suggestedCriteria}` for criteria judge. Confirmed.

### 🟡 step-3: Commit the most recent eval run as baseline and create eval-thresholds.json — medium risk
- The baseline file `backend/build/eval-results/eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` exists locally but is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent needs to copy its content to `eval-baseline.json` at repo root, which is NOT gitignored. The file is accessible on disk but may confuse agents that check git status.
- The baseline only contains 18 results (12 draft-assist + 6 lint). The fixture files define 52 total cases across 6 eval types. Future eval runs will have ~52 results. The regression comparison in step 4 must handle different population sizes, but step 3 doesn't call this out.
- The thresholds config references `judge_high_stakes.applies_to: ['RISK_SIGNAL', 'TEAM_INSIGHT', 'PERSONAL_INSIGHT', 'RAG_QUERY']` and `judge_assistive.applies_to: ['COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST', ...]`. But the eval results don't have an explicit 'type' field — only `caseId` prefix (e.g., `draft-`, `lint-`) and `promptVersion` (e.g., `commit-draft-assist-v1`). The enforcement script (step 4) must infer type from these, which is underspecified.

### 🟡 step-4: Build the threshold enforcement script — medium risk
- The plan says to use `path.resolve(__dirname, '..')` for repo root. This works for `scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` since `__dirname` is `<repo>/scripts/`. Correct.
- The eval result JSON does NOT have a per-result `passed` boolean field. There is only a top-level `passed` count. The plan correctly describes computing pass/fail from `schemaValid` and `checks`, but the agent must implement this rather than expecting a `passed` field.
- Type classification for `judge_high_stakes` vs `judge_assistive` threshold application is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in `eval-thresholds.json` reference type names like `COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST` but eval results use `promptVersion: 'commit-draft-assist-v1'` or caseId prefix `draft-`. The plan doesn't specify the mapping. For the initial implementation, title/criteria judge scores only exist on draft-assist cases so global means work, but faithfulness thresholds require type splitting.
- The baseline has 18 cases; future runs have 52. Regression checks comparing overall pass rates will compare different populations. The script should ideally compare per-caseId pass/fail for regression, but the plan describes high-level rate comparisons.

### 🟢 step-5: Split CI into two jobs: eval run (manual/allow_failure) + threshold check (hard-gate), and add npm scripts — low risk
- The plan says `eval:threshold-check` should have `rules` matching `eval:llm-judge` with `when: on_success`. In GitLab CI, with `needs: ['eval:llm-judge']` and `eval:llm-judge` being manual, the threshold-check job will be in 'created' state until the manual job completes. This is correct behavior.
- The `node:20-bookworm` image does not have npm/node_modules from the repo. But the script uses only Node 20 built-ins (fs, path, etc.) so no `npm ci` is needed. Correct.
- Adding `eval:run` and `eval:check` npm scripts to package.json is straightforward.

### 🔴 step-6: Add unit tests for the enforcement script and verify end-to-end — high risk

⚠️ **Checks will fail:** The `unit` npm script (`npm run test`) ends with `cd backend && ./gradlew test`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` runs from the `backend/` directory, so the glob resolves to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The node command will fail with 'no test files found' or similar error.
- CRITICAL: The current `test` npm script is `npm run test -w frontend -- --run && npm run test -w @weekly-commit/shared -- --run && cd backend && ./gradlew test`. After `cd backend && ./gradlew test` completes, the shell working directory is `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` will resolve the glob relative to `backend/`, looking for `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. The `unit` requiredCheck will FAIL. Fix: must include `cd ..` before the node command, e.g., `&& cd .. && node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js`.
- The test file uses `require('../eval-threshold-check.js')` to import exported functions. Step 4 must have the CommonJS exports and `require.main === module` guard working correctly for this to succeed. This is a cross-step dependency that is properly sequenced (step 4 before step 6).
- Node 20's built-in test runner (`node:test`) uses `describe`/`it` or `test()` syntax. The agent needs to use the correct API. This is well-documented but agents sometimes confuse it with Jest.

## Critical Issues (will cause failure)

- 🔴 Step 6: npm `test` script `cd backend` leaves cwd in `backend/`. Appending `&& node --test scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` resolves the glob to `backend/scripts/__tests__/*.test.js` which does not exist. Must insert `&& cd ..` before the node command. The `unit` requiredCheck WILL fail as written.

## Warnings (may cause problems)

- 🟡 Step 4: Type classification for judge thresholds is underspecified. The `applies_to` arrays in eval-thresholds.json reference types like 'COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST' but eval results only have `promptVersion` ('commit-draft-assist-v1') and caseId prefix ('draft-'). Agent must infer the mapping.
- 🟡 Step 3: The baseline `eval-2026-03-26T16-33-06.574104Z.json` only has 18 results (lint + draft-assist), while fixture files define 52 cases across 6 eval types. Regression pass-rate comparison across different population sizes is misleading unless done per-case.
- 🟡 Step 2: ~80-100 lines of new code (HTTP client, JSON parsing, template substitution) added to PromptEvalRunner.java must pass Eclipse spotless formatter. Agent should run `./gradlew spotlessApply` after editing.
- 🟡 Step 3: The source baseline file at `backend/build/eval-results/` is gitignored (`**/build/` in .gitignore). The agent must copy content from the local disk file, not from git. This works but could confuse agents that rely on git-tracked files only.

content.thinking:
**Evaluating material bugs**

I need to look for material bugs. It seems the latest *.json files are sorted by filename timestamp, but their implementation sorts all .json files in the directory. I also need to handle missing judge scores correctly by skipping checks and noting when scores are unavailable. Currently, the output lacks a note for missing scores, which is a requirement. I need to decide if I should fix this, as it may be considered material. Let's inspect and fix directly.

**Identifying potential bugs**

I'm checking for missing evaluation results and potential errors, and I see some warnings for missing baselines, which is good. However, I noticed a few issues. The computeMetrics groups all numeric scores, including non-judge scores, while checkThresholds only uses configured dimensions. There might be bugs related to critical case pass rates, as well as how current results and totals are printed. Additionally, the sorting of JSON files isn’t timestamp-based, which could lead to issues. I need to ensure clarity in how the results are handled, especially zero divisions.

**Addressing potential bugs**

I noticed a potential bug where skipping occurs if there are no results for the baseline. I might need to run tests with a fabricated sample to verify if judge thresholds are correctly grouped by type. It could be beneficial to add a note when no judge scores are available, either globally or by type. Adding a soft check for when no thresholds are produced is a good idea, so I can track if any judge checks were added and note when scores are unavailable.

**Evaluating scoring requirements**

I need to handle the missing judge scores note. I can do this without changing exports. However, upcoming unit tests might focus on checkThresholds. Right now, there aren't any tests. It could be more robust to include a soft check object from checkThresholds, but maybe that isn't essential. I should consider the scope and aim for minimal edits. Since exported functions should be testable, it makes sense for checkThresholds to show any skipped notes. I'll look into the context of the checkThresholds code block.

**Implementing minimal edits**

I want to make minimal edits in computeMetrics by adding `resultCountByType` counts for inferred types. In checkThresholds, for each group or type, I’ll check if there are results using `const hasResultsForType = (metrics.resultCountsByType?.[type] || 0) > 0`. If scores are present, I’ll push a check and set `addedAnyChecksForType` to true. After the loop, if there are results but no checks were added, I’ll note that judge scores are unavailable. This is relevant for types like COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST and COMMIT_LINT, where judge dimensions are not configured.

**Evaluating score checks**

I’m looking at how to handle missing scores for each dimension by tracking them with `missingCount++`. After looping through, if there are results for that type and if I haven’t checked any counts, I’ll throw in a note that says judge scores are unavailable. If there are any missing checks, I’ll mention that too. This approach seems ideal, but I’ll need to ensure `resultCountsByType` is included in `computeMetrics`. Time to patch that and maybe run a node script to check the notes!

I also need to handle missing judge scores correctly by skipping checks and noting when scores are unavailable. Currently, the output lacks a note for missing scores, which is a requirement. I need to decide if I should fix this, as it may be considered material. Let's inspect and fix directly."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Identifying potential bugs**

I'm checking for missing evaluation results and potential errors, and I see some warnings for missing baselines, which is good. However, I noticed a few issues. The computeMetrics groups all numeric scores, including non-judge scores, while checkThresholds only uses configured dimensions. There might be bugs related to critical case pass rates, as well as how current results and totals are printed. Additionally, the sorting of JSON files isn’t timestamp-based, which could lead to issues. I need to ensure clarity in how the results are handled, especially zero divisions."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Addressing potential bugs**

I noticed a potential bug where skipping occurs if there are no results for the baseline. I might need to run tests with a fabricated sample to verify if judge thresholds are correctly grouped by type. It could be beneficial to add a note when no judge scores are available, either globally or by type. Adding a soft check for when no thresholds are produced is a good idea, so I can track if any judge checks were added and note when scores are unavailable."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Evaluating scoring requirements**

I need to handle the missing judge scores note. I can do this without changing exports. However, upcoming unit tests might focus on checkThresholds. Right now, there aren't any tests. It could be more robust to include a soft check object from checkThresholds, but maybe that isn't essential. I should consider the scope and aim for minimal edits. Since exported functions should be testable, it makes sense for checkThresholds to show any skipped notes. I'll look into the context of the checkThresholds code block."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Implementing minimal edits**

I want to make minimal edits in computeMetrics by adding `resultCountByType` counts for inferred types. In checkThresholds, for each group or type, I’ll check if there are results using `const hasResultsForType = (metrics.resultCountsByType?.[type] || 0) > 0`. If scores are present, I’ll push a check and set `addedAnyChecksForType` to true. After the loop, if there are results but no checks were added, I’ll note that judge scores are unavailable. This is relevant for types like COMMIT_DRAFT_ASSIST and COMMIT_LINT, where judge dimensions are not configured."},{"type":"summary_text","text":"**Evaluating score checks**

I’m looking at how to handle missing scores for each dimension by tracking them with `missingCount++`. After looping through, if there are results for that type and if I haven’t checked any counts, I’ll throw in a note that says judge scores are unavailable. If there are any missing checks, I’ll mention that too. This approach seems ideal, but I’ll need to ensure `resultCountsByType` is included in `computeMetrics`. Time to patch that and maybe run a node script to check the notes!"}]}

content.text:
Reviewed `scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` against the step requirements and ran the required check.

What I reviewed:
- Exported API: `computeMetrics`, `checkThresholds`, `checkRegression`
- CommonJS + `require.main === module` guard
- Type inference from `promptVersion`
- Deterministic, judge, and regression handling
- CLI behavior and exit code
- Health-check relevance for this step

Material defect found:
- The script was silently skipping missing judge-score checks.
- Step 4 explicitly requires: missing judge scores should be skipped **and noted** as `"judge scores unavailable"`.
- In the current baseline-only run, no judge note was printed, so the implementation was incomplete.

What I changed:
- Updated `computeMetrics()` to track `resultCountsByType`
- Updated `checkThresholds()` to emit soft informational checks when a type has results but required judge scores are unavailable:
  - full unavailable: `judge scores unavailable — checks skipped`
  - partial unavailable: `some judge scores unavailable — N judge check(s) skipped`

Verification:
- `node scripts/eval-threshold-check.js` now prints judge-score-unavailable notes
- `cd backend && ./gradlew build` ✅ passed

Assessment:
- The step is now correctly implemented.
- The health-check concern relevant to this step (judge-score handling visibility) is addressed.

```observations
- [low] eval-thresholds.json / eval-baseline.json: The current baseline still hard-fails `overall_case_pass_rate` at 94.4% vs required 95.0%. That appears intentional for now, but CI will block once this script is wired in unless the baseline, thresholds, or failing case behavior changes.
- [medium] package.json / future step 6: The health-check warning about `cd backend && ./gradlew test && node --test ...` still appears valid and should be fixed when step 6 is implemented.
```

Parent conversation handoff summary

Overview:
ST6 is an enterprise software company enabling weekly commit-based planning with AI assistance. The session discussed how to make AI integral to the product (not bolted-on) and reviewed an evaluation pipeline for AI quality. We concluded the next milestone is implementing enforceable AI quality thresholds via: adding a critical field to eval cases, wiring two existing LLM judge prompts (title-quality, criteria-quality) into the PromptEvalRunner, committing the most recent eval run as a regression baseline, creating an eval-thresholds.json config, building a Node.js enforcement script that compares results against thresholds and baseline, and integrating that into CI.

Settled decisions:
- Judge thresholds must be 3-layer: deterministic gates first (schema+case pass rate), LLM judge absolute thresholds second, regression relative thresholds third.
- Split judge thresholds by risk: high-stakes features (risk signals, insights, RAG) get stricter thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.90) than assistive features (draft, lint, suggestions) which get more permissive thresholds (faithfulness >= 0.85).
- Deterministic thresholds are hard-gates that block CI; judge and regression thresholds initially soft-gate (report but don't block) until stable.
- Use eval-baseline.json as regression anchor committed to the repo. When eval runs, the enforcement script compares the latest results to both the config thresholds and the baseline snapshot.
- Judge scoring in the eval pipeline is optional: if OPENROUTER_API_KEY is not set, judge dimensions are skipped, not failed.
- The enforcement script will be a standalone Node.js script (scripts/eval-threshold-check.js) using only Node 20 built-ins, runnable outside Gradle.
- After step 2, the title-quality and criteria-quality judge prompts will be wired into PromptEvalRunner for draft-assist cases to produce real judge scores for calibration.

Constraints / must-remember items:
- All work is backend-test-only for steps 1-2; do not modify any main or frontend source files during those steps.
- Java code must pass spotlessCheck (Eclipse JDT formatter).
- PromptEvalRunner parameterized test structure must be preserved, not replaced.
- FaithfulnessEvaluator.java (runtime judge, separate from eval pipeline) must not be modified.
- Eval runner is @Tag("eval") and excluded from normal test runs — preserve that behavior.
- Judge scoring must be optional: skip if provider unavailable (no API key), don't fail.
- Eval results write to backend/build/eval-results/ — keep that path.
- Step 3 requires exactly 6 test cases marked as critical: 3 in draft-assist (draft-001, draft-004, draft-008) and 3 in commit-lint (lint-001, lint-003, lint-004).
- Regression thresholds must be configurable via eval-thresholds.json, not hardcoded in script logic.

Open questions:
- Should the enforcement script exit 0 or 1 when judge scores are unavailable (API key missing)? Currently plan says skip those checks and pass on deterministic, but CI teams may want a clear signal.
- The eval-baseline.json in step 3 references an old run timestamp (2026-03-26). Should we run a fresh eval before committing baseline to ensure the cases are stable?
- How should the enforcement script integrate with existing Gradle eval tasks — should it be invoked inside build.gradle or externally in CI?
- Do we want the threshold config to be versioned per prompt-version (e.g., separate thresholds for commit-draft-assist-v1 vs v2), or single global thresholds for all versions of a suggestion type?

Use this handoff as background context. If it conflicts with the explicit task below or the current repository state, prioritize the explicit task and repo state.
